title: Misbehaving: The Making of Behavioral Economics
excerpt: The inside story of the behavioral economics revolution, told by its founding father — a witty, personal account of how psychology invaded economics and changed our understanding of how people actually make decisions.
keyIdeas: Humans are not the rational 'econs' of classical economics — we are predictably irrational in systematic ways
Mental accounting explains why we treat money differently depending on its source and intended use
Present bias causes us to overweight immediate gratification at the expense of long-term welfare
Choice architecture can harness cognitive biases to improve decisions without restricting freedom
keyTakeaways: The gap between economic theory and actual human behavior is the founding insight of behavioral economics
Small changes in how choices are presented can produce large changes in outcomes
Self-control problems are not character flaws but predictable features of human cognition
Markets do not always correct irrational behavior — sometimes they exploit it
whoShouldRead: Economists and finance professionals open to questioning traditional assumptions
Policy makers interested in evidence-based interventions
Anyone who wants to understand their own financial blind spots
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a traditional textbook on behavioral economics
Those who prefer abstract theory over personal anecdotes and academic history
